Professor: Vassil Rousser
Department: Computer Science
University: University of New Orleans
Source: Rate My Professor
URL: https://www.ratemyprofessors.com/professor/136536

Courses:
CSCI 4621 - Intro Cyber Security
CSCI 4311 - Computer Networks & Telecomm
CSCI 4401 - Principles Operating Systems I

Total reviews:

Review:
Course: CSCI 4621 - Intro Cyber Security
Overall Rating: 2.0
Difficulty: 4.0
Would Take Again: no
Even though the professor do teach and I do appreciate that he tries to branch out to real-world things, the class is overall unorganized. We haven't gotten a single HW after midterm and he doesn't really pace well. The tests have little to do with the lectures and some parts may not even be in the book. Also this is for CSCI 4623.

Professor: Vassil Rousser
Department: Computer Science
University: University of New Orleans
Source: Rate My Professor
URL: https://www.ratemyprofessors.com/professor/136536

Courses:
CSCI 4621 - Intro Cyber Security
CSCI 4311 - Computer Networks & Telecomm
CSCI 4401 - Principles Operating Systems I

Total reviews:

Review:
Course: CSCI 4621 - Intro Cyber Security
Overall Rating: 1.0
Difficulty: 5.0
Would Take Again: no
This professor's approach is very inconsistent and unfair. Most stuff on his exams weren't covered in his lectures, which makes studying feels pointless. The exams are entirely short answer and he grades it very harshly with no clear rubric.Some students exams will receive a curve and some who needs it the most wouldn't.

Professor: Vassil Rousser
Department: Computer Science
University: University of New Orleans
Source: Rate My Professor

CSCI 4401 - Principles Operating Systems I

Total reviews:

Review:
Course: CSCI 4621 - Intro Cyber Security
Overall Rating: 1.0
Difficulty: 5.0
Would Take Again: no
Very boring and monotone lectures. You'll never see your assignments he gave you at the beginning of the semester and the week before the Finals. There's no way he's the CS department head when there are way better professors who could probably do his job better and actually know how to teach. PLEASE save yourself and take someone else!

Review:
Course: CSCI 4311 - Computer Networks & Telecomm
Overall Rating: 1.0
Difficulty: 5.0
Would Take Again: no
Some of the most boring and stale lectures with no coordination or planning for homework and tests. Expect a boring lecture daily, with one midterm and final. The teacher doesn't seem to care, just lecturing with vague, ungraded homework. There's nothing going on in the class

Professor: Vassil Rousser
Department: Computer Science
University: University of New Orleans
Source: Rate My Professor
URL: https://www.ratemyprofessors.com/professor/136536

Courses:
CSCI 4621 - Intro Cyber Security
CSCI 4311 - Computer Networks & Telecomm
CSCI 4401 - Principles Operating Systems I

Total reviews:

Review:
Course: CSCI 4401 - Principles Operating Systems I
Overall Rating: 4.0
Difficulty: 4.0
Would Take Again: yes
Read the textbook or pay close attending in class. Attendance isn't recorded and it may take all semester for grades to come back. Go to him early and ask for help if you need. He is a caring professor, but we are adults and shouldn't need to be spoon-fed like children. Start on the projects early and ask questions to get a focus for test material.

Professor: Vassil Rousser
Department: Computer Science
University: University of New Orleans
Source: Rate My Professor
URL: https://www.ratemyprofessors.com/professor/136536

Courses:
CSCI 4621 - Intro Cyber Security
CSCI 4311 - Computer Networks & Telecomm
CSCI 4401 - Principles Operating Systems I

Total reviews:

Review:
Course: CSCI 4401 - Principles Operating Systems I
Overall Rating: 4.0
Difficulty: 4.0
Would Take Again: yes
Lectures are good, but can be dry and homework assignments are reasonable, but still challenging. The hardest part are the tests. For essay questions, be very specific and wordy. For math questions, don't worry about finding the exact numerical value, rather think logically about the problem and show your process.

Dr Roussev there are a lot of job out there you don't have to teach. teaching is not for you.

Overall Rating: 5.0

Would Take Again: yes
Dr. Holmberg is an amazing professor. I had him for Systems Programming as well and he hasn't changed. He has flexible student hours and is very informative in his teaching. Very respectful and understanding. A good bit of homework, and a few projects.

One of the nicest enthusiastic teachers I've ever had. When ever you ask him a question, he gives extremely vivid happy/caring feedback. He explains everything in fine nitty gritty detail.Only negative is sometimes he talks a bit too much giving the class a static feeling making it hard to focus especially for me with ADHD. Still amazing and caring

Department: Computer Science
University: University of New Orleans

CSCI 1583 - Software Design and Development I

Total Reviews:

Review:
Course: CSCI 1583 - Software Design and Development I
Overall Rating: 5.0
Difficulty: 3.0
Would Take Again: yes
Ted is one of the most inspirational teachers I've had at UNO. He has a deep passion for teaching and software development, and every one of his lectures is fun. He encourages you to bring your laptop to class and code along with him, as he doesn't just read off powerpoint slides. I would take him again, but I think he currently only teaches 1583.

Professor: James Wagner
Department: Computer Science
University: University of New Orleans
Source: Rate My Professor
URL: https://www.ratemyprofessors.com/professor/2184393

Courses:
CSCI 4401 - Principles Operating Systems I
CSCI 4125 - Data Models and DBS Syst
CSCI 5501 - Programming Language Structure

Total reviews:

Review:
Course: CSCI 4401 - Principles Operating Systems I
Overall Rating: 5.0
Difficulty: 3.0
Would Take Again: yes
Attendance is mandatory, and the professor will record it manually. Both the midterm and final exams are open-book. There are 4+1 projects (with the last project counting as extra credit for the final). As long as you diligently complete the assignments and review the quizzes, you can easily got A. Reviewing the slides is the best way to study.

The mid-term and final exams are open-book exams. There are 10 phase projects, many of which offer extra credit. As long as you diligently do your assignment, you can easily get an A. Reviewing the slides is the best way to study.

He is the worst ever teacher. I have ever seen. You will het nothing no science no grade. He is very bad in teaching and delivering the knowledge. I hope that I will not see him again not taking a class with him. I had very hard time with him, he always under estimate the student in front of him.

He gave like 35 points of bonus credit on the final exam (a super easy extra credit assignment that he gave every a 25/25 on even if they did it wrong...) and undergrads always are given 10 bonus points. I didn't finish my last 2 assignments and got 90/95 on both. He also gives bonus credit on one of the four assignments. Easy professor and class

His lectures are boring and difficult to follow, but following his lectures is the only way to learn the material that will be on the quizzes and exams. His lecture slides are terrible. Midterm grades: #students-grade 11-A 7-B 3-C 6-D 13-F 33% of students made an unsatisfactory grade.

Unless you have home/work responsibilities, DO NOT take him for the online section. He is not forgiving if you miss anything or need extra help. Ask the discord instead. No late work is accepted. STRICT attendance

Want to actually learn ML? You'll learn nothing, maybe just some ML buzz words. A community college could probably teach us more about ML than him. Ng's CS
to actually learn ML? You'll learn nothing, maybe just some ML buzz words. A community college could probably teach us more about ML than him. Ng's CS229 >>>

Total Reviews:

Review:
Course: CSCI 4452 - Cloud Computing
Overall Rating: 5.0
Difficulty: 4.0
Would Take Again: yes
I cannot tell you in 350 characters how amazing this class was for me. This is the single most useful class I took in UNO. everything Manuel did from start to finish was PERFECT. Presentations helped students who were behind learn from other students. No exams, just a final project. This class was actually so much fun. I will never forget him.

Review:
Course: CSCI 4452 - Cloud Computing
Overall Rating: 2.0
Difficulty: 4.0
Would Take Again: no
He's a cool guy but this class is easily one of the most busywork classes you'll take. A bunch of homework, a bunch of presenting, and occasional random quizzes. If you don't show up to class you will not have a good time. He is accessible outside of class, and does give good feedback for assignments.

He knows what he's talking about, answers questions in a way that's understandable to someone with no knowledge beforehand of computer science, and is helpful outside of class. Definitely wants his students to succeed. Some test questions aren't discussed at all in lecture and are only in the textbook, so you have to read it. LOTS of extra credit.

Professor: Christopher Summa
Department: Computer Science

Total reviews:

Review:
Course: CSCI 2125 - Data Structures and Algorithms 
Overall Rating: 5.0

Would Take Again: yes
Amazing professor. Will explain things and concepts as many times as you need. Tests are straightforward, but they really do test your understanding of lecture so don't expect to fly by this class. Take notes, ask questions. Professor was available for office hours and through email. He also posts and records all lectures, even in person
s class. Take notes, ask questions. Professor was available for office hours and through email. He also posts and records all lectures, even in person ones.

He's a great teacher and explains things very clearly. In order to do well you must study everything he teaches crystal clear 100% especially the study guide. Tests are all short answer. Only negative is he gives a slight feeling like he's burnt out or tired of teaching and comes off rude sometimes. Overall fantastic for learning and gre
ly negative is he gives a slight feeling like he's burnt out or tired of teaching and comes off rude sometimes. Overall fantastic for learning and great teacher

Professor: Christopher Summa
Department: Computer Science
University: University of New Orleans
Source: Rate My Professor
URL: https://www.ratemyprofessors.com/professor/1697892

Courses: 
CSCI 2125 - Data Structures and Algorithms 
CSCI 2120 - Software Design and Development II
CSCI 1583 - Software Design and Development I

Total reviews:

Review:
Course: CSCI 2125 - Data Structures and Algorithms 
Overall Rating: 2.0
Difficulty: 4.0
Would Take Again: no
Exams are hard because the material is hard. Harder than Java 1-2. Other institutions tend to offer this course near the junior/ senior point of curriculum. At UNO it's a per-requisite for MOST C.S classes. You must pass in order to progress. Do all of the assignments, don't miss out on the points, use them as a pillow for the midterm and
OST C.S classes. You must pass in order to progress. Do all of the assignments, don't miss out on the points, use them as a pillow for the midterm and final.

Professor: Christopher Summa
Department: Computer Science
University: University of New Orleans
Source: Rate My Professor
URL: https://www.ratemyprofessors.com/professor/1697892

Courses: 
CSCI 2125 - Data Structures and Algorithms 
CSCI 2120 - Software Design and Development II
CSCI 1583 - Software Design and Development I

Total reviews:

Review:
Course: CSCI 2125 - Data Structures and Algorithms 
Overall Rating: 3.0
Difficulty: 4.0
Would Take Again: yes
Summa is a good professor. However, due to the way classes are scheduled, it is a lot of material; do review a week before the exam again to clear any confusion. This course is not like Java I or II. Set aside a day to dedicate to reviewing the material. The homework is difficult and does not really get asked in the exam. Do not count on
a I or II. Set aside a day to dedicate to reviewing the material. The homework is difficult and does not really get asked in the exam. Do not count on a curve.

Source: Reddit
Subreddit: r/UNO
Thread: UNO vs Loyola, Computer science
URL: https://www.reddit.com/r/NewOrleans/comments/a4qvpi/uno_vs_loyola_computer_science/

Discussion:
Ok, question to all of you out there that went to UNO for computer science (I know they're are a few here), or went to Loyola and work in the tech industry (if you exist here). Ok, I want to switch into a computer science major, I'm currently a bio major/premed, and I realized I made a mistake and after taking computer science classes (nine credits worth) I want to switch. Ok, so Loyola just started their program, and I really don't know how to gauge it. My question is: should I just transfer to UNO because it is an established program with connections to local companies or just stay at Loyola and try for internships o

hould I just transfer to UNO because it is an established program with connections to local companies or just stay at Loyola and try for internships on my own (they're working on getting connections like UNO has, it's literally a brand new degree). I know that UNO has the deal with GE. I'm kinda leaning towards UNO, but I love being at Loyola. Tell me your input Reddit folks.

UNO comp sci program is pretty decent, especially their information assurance program. You'll be able to get a solid job out of it.

It's been rebranded as a cyber security program!

Loyola is a waste for most things that aren't law.

Uno is pretty damn good for csci. There's a bio informatics route there that would interest you. Cyber security abounds as well. Uno is a wise choice. And cheap.

Since you're getting i

here's a bio informatics route there that would interest you. Cyber security abounds as well. Uno is a wise choice. And cheap.

Since you're getting into Information Assurance, I strongly recommend going to the NOLASEC meetings every month. There's someone who posts an invite each month on here, so keep an eye out for that. Classes can't cover everything in technology so take some time to see what's out there.

You may also want to get into bioinformatics considering you have biology experience. Fortunately UNO has classes for both.

Save your money, go to UNO. You might want to consider biomedical/mechanical engineering since you like medicine

Isn’t Loyola pretty expensive? I can’t imagine many scenarios where the education you get there for comp sci would be better than starting your ad

ne

Isn’t Loyola pretty expensive? I can’t imagine many scenarios where the education you get there for comp sci would be better than starting your adult life with all that saved tuition in your pocket.

Loyola gave me lots of scholarship money, so debt is not worry. That being said, I think I am leaning towards switching to UNO just because of it being an established program. Also, Loyola is about to be on probation for their accreditation (again); so who even knows if the program will be gutted.

I graduated from Loyola with a BS in Computer Science before they cut the program back in 2006. It was incredibly dumb and short sighted of them to cut the program in the first place, we even voiced our concerns back then. I do well for myself in IT but I know of others in my class who didn't or

o cut the program in the first place, we even voiced our concerns back then. I do well for myself in IT but I know of others in my class who didn't or couldn't follow through with finding a job in the tech field after graduation.

I still have these nightmares about Comp Sci at UNO. It was my senior year, and I took an "Intro to Comp Sci" class as a blowoff (I saved my electives for last). This class was so easy that I literally never went to class, just showed up for the tests. Anyway, I have this recurring nightmare that I accidentally got the date wrong for the exam and ended up missing it and failed the class. I think this has something to do with the fact that it also had a bullshit lab component that I was SUPPOSED to be there for. I showed up for the first and last one, and hand in

o do with the fact that it also had a bullshit lab component that I was SUPPOSED to be there for. I showed up for the first and last one, and hand in my work (the final was 'create an excel spreadsheet comparing different computers and describe what would go into your thought process for purchasing one') and the dude tells me that he has to fail me for the lab because I was supposed to be there for 75% of the classes. And I'm like "dude, holy crap, I didn't understand that part, I know this shit, I been doing this shit, look at this" and handed him my presentation (which was very well done) and he was like "ahhh, ok, no prob".

The class was like "this is a computer, this is the screen, and this is the 'keyboard' and thiiisss is a 'c.p.u.'". LOL I guess if I had never seen a computer befor

like "this is a computer, this is the screen, and this is the 'keyboard' and thiiisss is a 'c.p.u.'". LOL I guess if I had never seen a computer before, or it was like 1989, it would've been a great class.

Source: Reddit
Subreddit: r/UNO
Thread: CS at UNO
URL: https://www.reddit.com/r/UNO/comments/1o5uoo/cs_at_uno/

Discussion:
I'm a senior in high school and am just wondering what the CS course at UNO is like. I'm looking into UNO and LaTech and want to know which would be better for me. Any thoughts?

I know nothing of the computer world, but I went to Tech and UNO. Was going to UNO when Katrina hit, and then went to Tech for a year until I went back to UNO, where I graduated.

Anyway, the college experience at Tech is far and away better. UNO is a commuter school, and the campus life is nearly dead. The town of Ruston feels like it is half made up of college students, and it's a great place if you want that type of experience.

Historically speaking this has been true. However, UNO has im

ade up of college students, and it's a great place if you want that type of experience.

Historically speaking this has been true. However, UNO has implemented residency requirements for first year students and have really tried to promote campus life in recent years.

Its still nowhere close to a school like LSU, but we don't have a football program.

I got my CS minor from UNO and I was pleased with the education I received. I can't compare it to Louisiana Tech, but the Network Security work they are doing at UNO is pretty well regarded.

If it is any help, I am a grad student at Georgia Tech and I got a full ride based upon good work at UNO. There is also a Louisiana Tech grad student in my department.

Again, I can't speak about LaTech, but UNO is a pretty nice campus right on the Lake

O. There is also a Louisiana Tech grad student in my department.

Again, I can't speak about LaTech, but UNO is a pretty nice campus right on the Lakefront. There's tons of stuff to do, but very little "campus" life, at least when I was there. On the plus side, it's not in the middle of nowhere like LaTech, but I think the "college experience" at LaTech may be better.

Thanks for the feedback! UNO is also within 20 minutes of my house so I am trying to weigh the pros and cons of going there. I was told Tech has better job placement but I think both schools would do me well.

It also kind of depends on what kind of person you are. If you have trouble focusing with all your shiftless friends around, then maybe getting away is good. If you have a good circle of friends and feel you can go to

trouble focusing with all your shiftless friends around, then maybe getting away is good. If you have a good circle of friends and feel you can go to school the place you're familiar with, then it might not be a problem. Some people, even those used to partying in a city like New Orleans for high school, can't really handle "small-town college life". It's kind of a personal decision in that respect.

I am a CS student at UNO and it is a pretty good program. They are about to revamp the curriculum a little and it should be even better after that. As u/mikelj said, the security program is very good. I know nothing of the La Tech program, but I'm not sure heading out of town is going to help you out that much. Also, the job situation in New Orleans is pretty good right now, including some gre

m not sure heading out of town is going to help you out that much. Also, the job situation in New Orleans is pretty good right now, including some great internship opportunities for students. Not sure how things are in Ruston, but I would guess they are not as good.

I got my undergraduate degree in CS from UNO and am currently pursuing my PhD in CS at UNO. The program is easily the best in the state. Its not just a "learn to program" kind of curriculum, you will understand the underlying concepts of Computer Science by the time you graduate.

Also, the Information Assurance program is one of the best in the country. We have a Reverse Engineering class that is one of probably 5 like it in the whole country where the students disassemble malware to understand how it works.

My only piece of

ring class that is one of probably 5 like it in the whole country where the students disassemble malware to understand how it works.

My only piece of advice if you are from New Orleans is to live on campus. I floundered my first go-round at UNO because I lived at home and basically treated it like high school 2.0.

Reverse Engineering was the best class I've ever taken.
